1: The second stone tablets are chiseled by Moses himself — a significant departure from the first tablets where YHWH both wrote and provided the stone (Exodus 31:18). After the covenant breach, Moses participates more actively in the renewal. The wooden chest (aron etz) here is presented as a simple portable container Moses makes himself, before the more elaborate Exod 25 ark with its gold overlay and mercy-seat design. Deuteronomy's simpler presentation is consistent with its focus on the essentials of covenant relationship rather than the cultic details of the Priestly tradition.
2: YHWH's promise to rewrite the broken tablets — "I will write on the tablets the words that were on the first tablets, which you broke" — is a covenant renewal after dissolution. The fact that the content is identical to the first tablets confirms that the Decalogue is YHWH's own covenant terms, which YHWH himself re-commits to. The broken tablets are not forgotten (YHWH references "which you broke") — covenant history, including its ruptures and renewals, is preserved rather than erased.
3: Moses's personal construction of the ark — "so I made an ark of acacia wood" — is Deuteronomy's distinctive account, differing from the Exodus tradition where Bezalel constructs it as part of the tabernacle project (Exodus 37:1). Deuteronomy focuses on Moses's personal role as covenant mediator: Moses is presented as doing everything spiritually essential himself. The acacia wood (shittim) was the standard construction material for sacred objects in the tabernacle — lightweight, hard, and found in the Sinai region.
4: The tablets are identified as "the Ten Commandments" (aseret haddevarim, literally "the ten words") — Deuteronomy's term for what is usually called the Decalogue (from Greek deka logoi). The dual emphasis — spoken from fire to the assembly, then written by the divine finger — establishes both the spoken and written forms of the covenant. The assembly-day reference (yom haqqahal): the Sinai covenant was given in a context of corporate public witness, not private revelation.
5: "There they remain" (sham yihyu) — the tablets placed in the ark and remaining there. The resting place of the ark in Solomon's temple (1 Kings 8:9: "there was nothing in the ark except the two tablets of stone that Moses had placed there at Horeb") establishes unbroken continuity from Sinai to the First Temple. The covenant document at the center of Israel's worship-space defined the nature of the covenant relationship: Israel's entire cult was built around housing and honoring YHWH's treaty-text with Israel.
6: The parenthetical notice of Aaron's death at Moserah (the parallel in Numbers 20:22-29 places it at Mount Hor) is one of several minor geographical variations between Deuteronomy and Numbers. Aaron's death marks the priestly succession: "Eleazar his son took over the priestly ministry" (yekhahein tachatav — the standard succession formula for the Aaronic priesthood). The notice establishes the continuity of the Levitical-Aaronic priesthood into the period when Deuteronomy was composed.
7: The itinerary from Moserah through Gudgodah to Jotbathah ("a land of flowing streams") is a parenthetical geographical notice. Jotbathah's description as a land of streams (nachalei mayim) may contrast with the wilderness's waterless character, suggesting YHWH's providential water-provision even in desert journeys. These brief geographical notes served as mnemonic markers for the listening audience, connecting the speech to locations in Israel's collective memory.
8: The Levitical separation is one of the most theologically important institutional decisions in Israel's history. The three functions of the tribe of Levi — carry the ark, stand before YHWH to minister, pronounce blessings in his name — constitute the full range of sacral service. "Stand before YHWH" (la'amod lifnei YHWH l'sharto) is the court-service formula used for royal attendants in ANE palace administration; here it describes the Levites as YHWH's divine court personnel. Pronouncing blessings "in his name" (bishemo l'varech) is the liturgical function described in the Aaronic blessing (Numbers 6:22-27), where the priest puts YHWH's name on Israel. The phrase "which they do to this day" signals Deuteronomy's awareness of ongoing Levitical ministry — the institutions described are still functioning.
9: "The LORD is his inheritance" (YHWH hu nachlatam) is the theological definition of the Levitical condition. While the other tribes received territorial inheritance, the Levites received YHWH himself — mediated through their share in altar offerings and tithes (Deuteronomy 18:1-8). This is a radical social arrangement: an entire tribe was removed from the land-economy and sustained by the covenant community's worship practices. The Levites' landlessness made them perpetually dependent on YHWH's provision through the community — institutionalized covenant dependence. The NT applies this pattern to Christian ministry: "the Lord has commanded that those who proclaim the gospel should get their living by the gospel" (1 Corinthians 9:14).
10: Moses's summary of the second forty-day fast confirms its intercessory character: "the LORD was unwilling to destroy you." The understatement is deliberate — the divine unwillingness to destroy is presented as the outcome of sustained prophetic intercession. The theological implication is that Moses's intercession genuinely influenced the divine decision, not by overcoming YHWH's will, but by activating a deeper level of YHWH's covenantal commitment. This is not prayer coercing God but prayer engaging the divine character that is already committed to covenant faithfulness.
11: "Get up and lead the people on their journey" — the command after the golden calf crisis is a covenant renewal and mission confirmation. YHWH does not revoke Moses's leadership or alter the destination; the covenant is renewed to the same ends. The Exodus-to-Canaan mission is re-commissioned, not scaled back. The theological point is the stability of divine purpose despite human failure: the golden calf did not permanently redirect Israel's covenant trajectory. This is the pattern Paul describes in Romans 3:3: "what if some were unfaithful? Does their faithlessness nullify the faithfulness of God?"
12: "What does the LORD your God ask of you?" — this summary formula is the closest Deuteronomy comes to a single-verse statement of the covenant's essence, comparable to Micah 6:8 ("what does YHWH require of you but to do justice, love covenant-faithfulness, and walk humbly with your God") and Mark 12:28-30 (Jesus's greatest commandment). Five verbs follow: fear (yirah), walk (halakh), love (ahavah), serve (oved), keep (shamar). The sequence moves from inner disposition (fear, love) to embodied practice (walk, serve, keep). This is not law-keeping as transaction but covenant relationship as total orientation of person.
13: "For your own good" (l'tovatekhah) — the commandments are not arbitrary impositions but provisions for human flourishing within the covenant structure. Deuteronomy consistently presents covenant obedience as in Israel's genuine interest, not merely YHWH's demand. The treaty-form context supports this: a suzerain's provisions protected the vassal's welfare as well as the suzerain's honor. This anticipates the NT principle that divine commands were intended to lead to life (Romans 7:10: "the commandment that was intended to bring life") — the intention was always human flourishing.
14: "To the LORD your God belong the heavens, even the highest heavens, the earth and everything in it" — the universal sovereignty claim that makes Canaan's gift more striking, not less. If YHWH owns everything, the gift of a specific land is a grant from the universal owner, not a property transfer. The phrase hashamayim u'shmei hashamayim (the heavens and the heavens of heavens) is a merism for the totality of the cosmos, appearing also in 1 Kings 8:27 (Solomon's temple dedication prayer) and Nehemiah 9:6. YHWH's universal ownership is the theological prerequisite for his ability to grant Canaan to Israel.
15: The contrast between YHWH owning everything (Deuteronomy 10:14) and choosing Israel specifically ("yet the LORD delighted in your fathers and loved them") creates a sovereign election framework. The verb hashaq (to delight in, to set affection upon) describes specific personal attachment — it is used of romantic attachment in Genesis 34:8. The patriarchal love (ahav) that motivated YHWH's choice is not earned love but initiated love: YHWH loved the patriarchs and then their descendants not because of something in them but because of something in himself. Deuteronomy 7:7-8 makes this explicit: "it was not because you were more in number ... but because YHWH loves you."
16: "Circumcise your hearts and stop being stubborn" (umaltem et-arlathem levavechem v'arpechem lo taqshu od): the metaphor of heart circumcision is one of Deuteronomy's most theologically provocative formulations. Physical circumcision was the covenant sign (Genesis 17:10-14) signifying belonging to Abraham's family. Heart circumcision uses the same image — the removal of an obstruction to full covenant intimacy — applied to the inner life. Orlat levav (foreskin of the heart) is the uncircumcised heart that cannot receive the covenant's demands. Jeremiah (Jeremiah 4:4) and Paul (Romans 2:29) both take up this metaphor, and Deuteronomy itself predicts that YHWH will accomplish this eschatologically (Deuteronomy 30:6: "YHWH your God will circumcise your heart ... to love YHWH your God with all your heart"). The command of 10:16 and the promise of 30:6 define the covenant's anthropological problem and divine solution.
17: "The LORD your God is God of gods and Lord of lords" (elohei ha'elohim u'adonei ha'adonim) — a superlative claim using the ANE idiom of stacked genitive constructions (king of kings, lord of lords) to intensify to the highest degree. This exact title ("Lord of lords") appears in Daniel 2:47 and is applied to Christ in Revelation 17:14 and Deuteronomy 19:16. The four divine attributes that follow — great, mighty, awesome, impartial, and incorruptible — contrast with ANE deities that could be manipulated through offerings and were favorable toward their ethnic in-group. YHWH's impartiality is a covenant guarantee: the powerless can rely on his justice because it is structurally incorruptible.
18: "He upholds justice for the fatherless and the widow, and loves the foreigner, providing them with food and clothing" — the classic triad of the marginalised in ANE covenant law (orphan, widow, sojourner). This triad appears throughout the prophets (Isaiah 1:17; Jeremiah 7:6; Zechariah 7:10; Malachi 3:5) as the test of covenant faithfulness: a society that protects its most vulnerable members shows that YHWH's covenant character has shaped its social structures. In the ANE, the fatherless and widow had no male protector in a patrilineal society; the foreigner (ger) had no tribal or kinship network. YHWH functions as the institutional patron of those who have no human patron — a revolutionary social theology embedded in covenant law.
19: "So love the foreigner, for you were foreigners in the land of Egypt" — the motivation for covenant care of the ger is Israel's own experience of vulnerability and dependence. This is Deuteronomy's characteristic move: historical experience becomes the basis for covenant ethics. The command to love (ahavtem) the foreigner uses the same verb as YHWH's love for the patriarchs (Deuteronomy 10:15) — the same love commanded of Israel toward the resident alien. The logic: YHWH loved you when you were powerless; therefore you must love those who are powerless. This is the ethical structure Jesus deploys in his summary of the law (Matthew 22:34-40) and Paul in his ethics (Romans 13:8-10).
20: The three-verb summary of covenant response — "fear the LORD your God, serve him, hold fast to him" — parallels the five-verb summary of Deuteronomy 10:12. The verb davaq (hold fast, cling to) is the covenant-loyalty term — it describes exclusive attachment (also used in Genesis 2:24 for the marital bond: "therefore a man shall leave his father and mother and hold fast to his wife"). Taking oaths in YHWH's name is the formal legal declaration of covenant identity: swearing by YHWH's name acknowledged his sovereignty over all truth-claims.
21: "He is your praise and he is your God" — tehillah (praise) applied to YHWH as Israel's proper object of praise means that YHWH himself is the content of Israel's praise, not merely the recipient. Israel praises YHWH by declaring what he has done, and what he has done constitutes the substance of their identity. The "great and awe-inspiring things for you that your own eyes have seen" refers to the Exodus and wilderness events — Israel's praise-vocabulary is entirely grounded in witnessed historical acts, not abstract theological claims.
22: "Your fathers went down to Egypt as seventy people, and now the LORD your God has made you as numerous as the stars of the sky" — the fulfillment of the Abrahamic promise (Genesis 15:5: "look toward heaven and number the stars, if you are able ... so shall your offspring be"). The seventy (Genesis 46:27; Exodus 1:5) who entered Egypt and the star-numerous multitude who left are placed in direct contrast — the Exodus is itself a fulfillment of the Abrahamic multiplication promise. This concludes the argument of chapters 8-10: Israel possesses nothing on the basis of merit, but everything on the basis of YHWH's covenantal faithfulness to sworn promises.
